phase_metadata.description:
Learn to interact with the file system, handle
persistent data storage, and convert objects into byte streams for storage or
transmission.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between Byte Streams and Character Streams

Perform efficient
file operations using BufferedReader and BufferedWriter

Master automatic
resource management with try-with-resources

Understand Object Serialization
and Deserialization using the Serializable interface

Explore modern Java NIO
(New I/O) using Path and Files classes

Manage file metadata and directory
structures
prerequisites:
Phase 9: Exception Handling (crucial for I/O
errors)

Phase 12: Java 8+ Features (useful for Files.lines() stream
processing)
introduction:
Up until now, all data handled by our programs
lived in the computer's volatile RAM and vanished once the program stopped. File
I/O (Input/Output) allows us to save data permanently on a disk. Java provides
two sets of tools: the classic 'java.io' package for stream-based I/O and the
modern 'java.nio' (New I/O) package for high-performance, path-based
operations.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
File I/O is the mechanism
of reading data from a source (Input) and writing data to a destination
(Output). Serialization is a specific type of I/O where a Java object's state is
converted into a byte stream.
concept_explanation.why_do_we_need_it:
We need I/O for
persistence (saving user settings, high scores, logs) and communication (sending
data over a network). Buffering is used to reduce the number of expensive disk
access operations by grouping data in memory.
concept_explanation.how_it_works:
Java uses
'Streams' to handle I/O. Think of a stream as a continuous flow of data. Byte
Streams (InputStream/OutputStream) handle raw binary data like images. Character
Streams (Reader/Writer) handle text data using Unicode.
concept_explanation.serialization_basics:
To make an object 'Serializable', its class must
implement the java.io.Serializable marker interface. This allows the
ObjectOutputStream to save the object's instance variables to a file.
concept_explanation.nio_path_files:
The NIO.2 API (introduced in Java 7) uses 'Path' objects to
represent file locations and the 'Files' utility class for powerful, one-line
operations like reading all lines or copying files.

syntax_breakdown.BufferedWriter:
A wrapper that provides a buffer for
efficient writing. Without it, every write() call would hit the disk
immediately.
syntax_breakdown.try_with_resources:
Ensures that the file stream is closed
automatically at the end of the block, even if an error occurs.
syntax_breakdown.transient_keyword:
Used in serialization to mark fields that should NOT be
saved (e.g., passwords or sensitive data).
syntax_breakdown.serialVersionUID:
A unique
identifier for a serializable class to ensure that a saved object and the
current class version are compatible.
syntax_breakdown.Path_of:
A modern static factory
method to create a Path object representing a file or directory.

rules_and_key_points:
Always close I/O streams to prevent memory leaks and
file locking.

Use Character Streams (Reader/Writer) for text files to handle
encoding correctly.

Use Byte Streams (InputStream/OutputStream) for binary
files like images and audio.

The 'transient' keyword prevents specific fields
from being serialized.

Static variables cannot be serialized because they
belong to the class, not the instance.

The Serializable interface is a
'Marker Interface' (it has no methods).

Files.exists(path) is the modern way
to check for a file's existence.

Buffered streams are significantly faster
than unbuffered streams for large files.

Serialization requires all nested
objects within the class to also be Serializable.

I/O operations almost
always throw IOException, which must be handled or declared.

code_example_1.title: Text File Writing and Reading
code_example_1.description: Using modern NIO.2 to write text and classic BufferedReader to read it back.

code_example_2.title: Object Serialization
code_example_2.description:
Saving and
loading a User object to a file, demonstrating the transient keyword.

code_example_2.question: What is the difference between File and Path?
code_example_2.answer:
File
is the older class from java.io. Path is the modern interface from java.nio.
Path is more flexible, supports different file systems, and works better with
the Files utility class.

code_example_2.question:
Why is the transient keyword
useful?
code_example_2.answer:
It provides security by preventing sensitive data like
passwords from being saved to disk and reduces file size by skipping unnecessary
or derived fields.

code_example_2.question:
Does serialization save static
variables?
code_example_2.answer:
No. Static variables belong to the class, not the
object. Serialization only captures the instance state.

code_example_1.summary:
Phase 14 introduced the concepts of data persistence. We learned how to read
and write text using both classic IO and modern NIO, managed resources safely
using try-with-resources, and used Serialization to save entire object states to
disk while protecting sensitive data with the transient keyword.
key_takeaways: IO = Data transfer; NIO = Modern, high-performance IO.
Character streams are for text; Byte streams are for binary.

Try-with-resources is the standard for closing
files.
Serializable is required for object storage.

Transient fields are
ignored by serialization.

Files.writeString and Files.readString are the easiest ways to handle text in
Java 11+.

Always
check if a file exists before reading to avoid exceptions.
code_example_1.preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 15, we will put everything we've learned
together to build real-world Mini Projects like a Student Management System and
a Banking Application.

questions.question:
What is the difference between a Reader and an
InputStream?
questions.answer:
An InputStream reads raw bytes (8-bit), while a Reader
reads characters (16-bit). Readers are specifically designed to handle text and
perform character encoding conversion automatically.

questions.question:
Explain the role of the
'serialVersionUID' in Java Serialization.
questions.answer:
It is a version number
for a Serializable class. When an object is deserialized, the JVM checks if the
ID in the file matches the ID in the class. If they don't match, an
InvalidClassException is thrown, preventing compatibility issues between
different versions of the code.

questions.question:
Write a snippet to read a file line-by-line
using Files.lines() and print only lines that contain the word 'Error'.

How would you append text to an
existing file using the 'Files' class without overwriting the previous
content?

Write a program to count the number of words in
a file named 'notes.txt'.
code_example_1.question:
Implement a method that
copies a binary file (like an image) from one path to another using
BufferedInputStream and BufferedOutputStream.

implement to be compatible with ObjectOutputStream?

What is the output of
writing 'Hello' with FileWriter and not calling close()?

options.B: The file might be empty or incomplete
